data.title: Goldman Sachs BDC's Risky Math: Buying The Discount, Ignoring The Drift
data.description:
When analyzing the Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD) in the context of the current market and macro conditions, several conflicting factors come into play. The pressures over the past few months have been amplified by the Iran war. However, signs of fatigue and unwinding in AI trades (markets uncomfortable with long-duration assumptions and high valuations) were already starting to show up since October last year. In such markets, BDCs do not function as defensives either because of leveraged credit positions by definition. So, the macro and market environment is not unusually supportive for GBDC. Additionally, we are already talking about a stickier-than-expected inflation regime ahead with slow growth, and stagflation risks are probably one of the biggest deterrents for BDCs with growing credit risks and little income growth. The rates scenario also potentially presents the worst of uncertainties, with credit stress concerns far from over (if rates remain higher for longer) and the declining overall rates cycle pressuring income for longer-term horizon investors.

GSBD is a high-quality and high-yield credit instrument, no doubt, and has been correcting in the recent past, reflecting some of the macro worries. The Q4 2025 results and commentary also show a mix of green shoots (like improvements in non-accruals) and underlying tensions that should be watched (like an increase in watchlist loans and reliance on non-cash).

Overall, the fundamentals and macro/market conditions do not look clean. However, this analysis looks into why the GSBD stock still becomes a buy, despite the not-so-perfect positioning. The core support comes from the ~30% discount it is trading at today. That, coupled with the fact that most of the fundamental stress ahead (in a realistic but aggressive stress scenario) appears to be cushioned by 2-3 years of even a stressed dividend (that’s actually strong for a credit asset), makes GSBD a buy on the overall valuation and income math.

Compressing Income - Return Math Intact

Let's start with the numbers that show what is actually happening already. Since the end of 2024, investment income has shrunk significantly - NII per share has fallen by over 20%, from ~$0.48 to ~$0.37. According to the management commentary in the Q4 2025 earnings call, yield on book has compressed from ~10.3% to ~9.9% during this period, which can be attributed to the rates normalization regime we are in (until stagflation risks started emerging recently). Along with a period of lower yields, expenses have not come down commensurately - they remain sticky around the ~$43m mark, which means the drop in yields and income flows through almost directly to the net income.

Dividends have also been cut (even after including the effects of a special dividend since 2025), as seen in the chart below. And all this even before any real credit stress has started to emerge.

GSBD's forward outlook also looks messy in the face of macro uncertainties. If rates continue to decline, income could be further pressured. On the other hand, if stagflation effects start showing up, credit stress becomes more relevant. Additionally, part of the current income (~9%) includes Payment in Kind (PIK), which is non-cash and could be linked to stressed or modified loans that could challenge durability. The overall environment appears to be one where GSBD is set up to best manage the portfolio rather than being in a phase where income expands and grows.

To account for a reasonably aggressive stress scenario and check whether GSBD's discount and income setup accounts for it, I have assumed that dividends settle somewhere meaningfully lower than the peak ~$0.53 per share seen earlier in 2025. Even if I assume stressors emerge (likely), and NII per share shrinks from ~$0.37 now to ~$0.25 (that is half of Q4 2024 levels), and if I assume that the NAV also drops by 30% (already at ~$12.64, down from ~$13.41 since Q4 2024) to ~$9, the recovery timelines for fresh investments today look more than good for a credit asset.

At say ~$0.25 quarterly dividends (NII almost fully paid out), the ~$3.5 drop in price at that point takes around 3 years to break even. That is as good as a cushion as you will get in the credit world for a scenario that is decently aggressive (assuming rates, credit, and income quality all start moving against the position at once for a strong portfolio with ~93% first lien exposure). I have even maintained the current discount setup in the scenario I discussed (in fact, slightly higher discount at ~32%), with a ~$3.5 drop in NAV corresponding to a similar drop in share prices.

This scenario is not an immediate possibility either, which reduces the ask of the return math calculations I have assumed. As of now, the ~$0.37 NII more than covers the ~$0.32 dividend obligations. Plus, GSBD reports a spillover of ~$0.97 per share to cover temporary setbacks and even keep yields at manageable levels for several quarters.

Mixed Credit Positioning

The credit picture can be read from both sides (supportive factors versus concerns) - signals are mixed. On the surface, things look better with non-accruals down to ~1.9%, from a peak of ~3.4% (and ~2% during the year). But there are signs indicating caution ahead. Watchlist loans have moved from 3.7% to 6% (defined as loans where risks have increased materially since origination, but payments are not more than 120 days past due). The majority of the book still sits in the stable bucket (no material change in risk since origination) but is down sequentially (~91.7%, down from 93%).

Overall, we are not yet talking about widespread credit stress as a caution -, especially looking at the non-accruals data improvement. But the jump in watchlist loans does warrant more monitoring. Especially, if the newer originations are contributing to the watchlist, while the older assets are performing well - that could be a worrying trend investors will need to keep an eye on.

Structurally, one of the supportive factors for GSBD's portfolio is the strong first lien exposure. Also, management constantly and proactively manages exposure, so risk management and underwriting discipline appear strong. For instance, management has reduced exposure to risky ARR loans from ~39% to ~11% through selective strategic exits and new underwriting filters (focus on EBITDA-based investments). GSBD also discussed how it has exited a performing loan at ~0.99 due to AI risk in the earnings call, emphasizing proactive AI risk management even if the numbers do not flag quantitatively. That matters in today's environment of uncertainty, where AI commoditizing several SaaS functionalities is the talk of the town. And software still is one of the largest sector exposures for GSBD (~17.6%). Overall growth prospects of portfolio companies are important to monitor (not only software) - if growth slows, there is not a lot of cushion as borrowers are fairly leveraged too (~5.9x leverage and ~2.0x interest coverage).

All in all, while not extremely concerning, I do not see the credit situation as a clean Buy signal - something that the non-accruals data suggests. A meaningful part of the portfolio (~43%) is still working through older vintages, adding uncertainty in the evolved macro situation new loans are originating from. While a lot of these transitions and macro effects are on my watchlist, there is a lot of structural strength in GSBD's proactive and qualitative risk management approach.

Valuation and Return Math Create Opportunity

Before concluding the thesis, let us look at the net debt-to-equity leverage, which has broadly trended up from ~1.17x in Q4 2024 to ~1.27x now. While not alarming, it shows that flexibility in the balance sheet is reducing, coinciding with a regime of slowing income. This doesn’t break the thesis, but it shows that the margin for error is narrowing.

Overall, as I mentioned at the start, fundamentals are not why I say Buy GSBD - it's the valuation and return math (buying the stock versus buying the business) that clinch the deal for fresh investments. What looks weak (when looking at the declining income, NAV, and a rising leverage) is more than compensated by a ~30% discount to NAV and a supportive yield under stress scenarios too. I keep credit also as a neutral factor at the moment, but should be monitored as signals are mixed.

GSBD is not a stable and fundamentally strong compounder at the moment, and for the current market and macro setup, it is a discounted credit vehicle where yield compensates for instability.

data.title: Nabors Industries: The War Is Only Bullish Once It Ends
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Nabors Industries (NBR) has approximately doubled since my prior article:

This probably makes it a good time to revisit the investment thesis, especially in view of the Iran war that is already extending beyond one month, with profound consequences for the global energy complex.

Energy stocks (XLE) have gone up in this time, though not nearly as much as oil prices (CL1:COM):

First-order thinking may suggest the war and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz are bullish for Nabors too. However, the situation is more nuanced, and I believe Nabors has more to gain from the cessation of hostilities than their continuation.

The longer the war goes on, the greater the risk of impacted energy infrastructure and suspended drilling operations. As Nabors has bet big on the SANAD joint venture with Saudi Aramco and also has a presence in neighboring Gulf countries, this means a potentially significant EPS hit. Payments from customers may also be at risk.

Conversely, the war's end will bring along demand for rebuilding the damage caused to the oil infrastructure, and that will likely result in Nabors' revenue from the region exceeding any pre-war expectations.

Ultimately, I don't have a geopolitical crystal ball and cannot predict when or how the war will end. That is why I have adjusted my Nabors stance to neutral. My preference currently is for oilfield services stocks that may do well under both the war ends / war continues scenarios.

Recap of the Recent Bullish Factors

In my view, there are several reasons why Nabors' stock started moving up. First, the Quail divestment I have discussed previously allowed meaningful deleveraging - net debt is now below 2.0x EBITDA for the first time in a long while:

Yields on Nabors' traded debt have fallen meaningfully:

Assuaging the debt concern has gone a long way in helping the equity "re-rate."

Second, Saudi Aramco - before the war - had started recalling drilling rigs that were suspended after the change in strategy in early 2024.

Third, U.S. oil rigs also appear to have bottomed, even if so far it has been only an L-shaped bottom, while U.S. gas rigs have been on the rise:

Fourth, and perhaps somewhat a bit irrationally, Nabors and other oilfield services stocks moved up a lot on the U.S. seizure of Venezuela's Maduro. While Venezuela's production seems to have gone up a notch, most observers remain skeptical about it reaching 3 million barrels per day any time soon.

The Gulf Hostilities Are a Double-Edged Knife

This brings us to the "elephant in the room", the war in the Gulf and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz that, by some estimates such as Rystad, has taken offline 12 million barrels per day of liquid supply. What is worse, these barrels aren't just accumulating in storage and waiting for the Strait to reopen but have effectively forced production shut-ins.

The market seems to put a premium on oil production - and by implication, oilfield services assets - that are located outside the region but isn't as bullish on assets that are trapped in the Gulf. Even though Nabors has gained over the last month, it is trailing Patterson-UTI (PTEN), a U.S.-focused drilling and completions company, by about 20 points, completely reversing the previous dynamic:

While Nabors would also benefit from its U.S. rigs, it has a much heavier presence in the Gulf region:

Besides the 60+ Saudi rigs, of which only 50% of EBITDA trickles down to Nabors equity holders given the JV, Nabors also runs rigs in Kuwait and Bahrain, two countries that have effectively shut in their entire production. Apart from the non-zero direct kinetic risk for the drilling operations, the bigger issue is that no one in their right mind would pay to drill new wells while they can't market existing production. The affected Gulf governments may even struggle with their fiscal budgets given they can't sell oil, so new drilling won't be their top priority.

On the Saudi side, there have already been significant war-related rig suspensions, but so far these appear to have been offshore only. Arabian Drilling, essentially a competitor to Nabors' SANAD, reported a few days ago that its Saudi land fleet of 39 rigs continues to operate at full capacity for now.

Nonetheless, Saudi Arabia also remains offtake constrained, not production constrained. According to Bloomberg, the Saudi East-West pipeline has now reached its full throughput capacity of 7 million barrels per day, but the limiting factor appears to be the Yanbu loading terminal capacity, which sits much lower at about 4.5 million barrels. In other words, there is zero incentive for Saudi Aramco to recall more rigs until the hostilities end and the Strait reopens.

My feeling is that the more this situation goes on, the more Nabors has to lose. Eventually there will be some supply response in the U.S., and that will lift utilizations and dayrates on the U.S. side, but Nabors' strategy has been "Saudi first" for a number of years now, and that is where the heavy capex has gone, which may have otherwise repaid debt.

In this sense, Nabors is a "Strait open" play right now, like most of the global economy. If the war continues, I expect Nabors, along with other Gulf-heavy oilfield services stocks, to stall or even sell off.

Additional Risks

Oil prices are extremely volatile right now, with the market doing 180-degree turns on any tweet from the administration or media report citing what some anonymous official thinks about the war situation. This magnifies the risk for Nabors too.

Second, if the broader market crashes, Nabors will likely also be affected.

Takeaway

I have been fundamentally bullish on Nabors for a long time now, but geopolitical risk had not been factored into my thesis. Right now, the geopolitical factor is so overwhelming that Nabors is becoming a bit of a binary Hormuz open/Hormuz closed trade.

As I can't predict the geopolitical outcomes, I prefer to sit on the sidelines. I am more interested right now in Nabors's competitor Patterson-UTI, which is a U.S.-focused play. I believe even after the Strait reopens, U.S. drilling activity will see some boost as the oil price floor will likely resettle higher when all this (hopefully) resolves.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
